name: Toci
description: Toci, meaning "Our Grandmother", was a prominent mother goddess figure in Aztec mythology. She was associated with healing, midwifery, herbalism, and the steam bath (temazcal). As a goddess of the earth, she represented fertility and was also connected to weaving and spinning. Toci held a complex and multifaceted role, embodying both nurturing and destructive aspects. She was sometimes depicted as an old woman, symbolizing wisdom and experience, but also as a fierce warrior goddess, reflecting her protective nature. She was also associated with the earth monster, Tlaltecuhtli, and was sometimes seen as an aspect of Coatlicue. Toci was revered as a patron of doctors and healers, and her festivals involved offerings of herbs and medicines. Her connection to the temazcal highlighted her role in purification and rebirth. In some accounts, she is considered the mother of Huitzilopochtli. Toci's duality reflects the Aztec understanding of the universe as a balance between opposing forces, where creation and destruction are intertwined. Her influence permeated various aspects of Aztec life, from medicine and childbirth to agriculture and warfare, solidifying her importance as a central figure in their religious beliefs.
subtitle: Our Grandmother, Goddess of Healing

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli
keyMyths.description: While often credited to Coatlicue, some traditions associate Toci with the miraculous birth of Huitzilopochtli, emphasizing her maternal role and connection to powerful deities. She demonstrates strength and resilience in the face of adversity.

keyMyths.title: Toci and the Temazcal
keyMyths.description: Toci's association with the temazcal, the traditional Aztec steam bath, highlights her role in purification, healing, and spiritual cleansing. The temazcal was a place of rebirth, symbolizing Toci's power to restore and rejuvenate.
keyMyths.source: Accounts of Aztec rituals and ceremonies

keyMyths.title: Toci as an Aspect of Coatlicue
keyMyths.description: In some interpretations, Toci is seen as an aspect or manifestation of Coatlicue, the earth goddess, emphasizing the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. This connection underscores Toci's powerful connection to the earth and its life-giving forces.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, flowers, and woven goods were made to Toci at her temples and shrines. These offerings were intended to honor her and seek her blessings for health, fertility, and a good harvest.

worshipPractices.name: Temazcal Rituals
worshipPractices.description: The temazcal, or steam bath, was an important part of Toci's worship. It was seen as a place of purification and rebirth, reflecting Toci's role as a mother goddess. Rituals within the temazcal were performed to seek healing and spiritual cleansing.

festivals.name: Ochpaniztli
festivals.timing: The eleventh month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: Ochpaniztli was a major festival dedicated to Toci and other earth goddesses. It involved sweeping rituals, offerings, and dances to ensure a bountiful harvest and to honor the earth's fertility. A female captive was often sacrificed, representing the shedding of the old and the renewal of the earth.

cultural.modernLegacy: Toci's influence can be seen in modern Mexican traditions related to midwifery and herbal medicine. While direct worship is no longer practiced, the respect for the earth and the importance of grandmothers in family life reflect her enduring legacy. Some contemporary healers draw inspiration from her association with traditional medicine.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Toci, meaning "Our Grandmother" in Nahuatl, emerges from the complex tapestry of Aztec cosmology as a primordial mother figure. While specific myths detailing her birth are scarce, she is generally understood to be a self-created deity, inherent to the earth itself. She is often associated with the earth goddess Coatlicue, sometimes even considered an aspect of her. As such, Toci embodies the inherent generative power of the earth, the source of all life. Her existence precedes the establishment of the Fifth Sun, the current era in Aztec cosmology, positioning her as an ancient and foundational force. Her role as "grandmother" signifies her age, wisdom, and the respect she commands as an ancestral figure. She is the wellspring from which the Aztec people, and all living things, draw sustenance.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Toci doesn't feature prominently in many dramatic narratives, her significance lies in her symbolic role. One important association is with the myth of Quetzalcoatl. In some accounts, she is considered the mother of Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent god, highlighting her role in nurturing and giving rise to important deities. Another story connects her to the creation of pulque, an alcoholic beverage made from agave. In this narrative, she is associated with Mayahuel, the goddess of agave, further emphasizing her connection to fertility and sustenance. These stories underscore her nurturing and life-giving properties. Although not always the central figure in elaborate tales, her presence is a constant reminder of the earth's power to both create and destroy.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Toci's worship was deeply interwoven with the agricultural cycle and the well-being of the community. Temples dedicated to her were centers of healing and midwifery. Priests and priestesses of Toci were skilled in herbal medicine and performed rituals to ensure successful childbirth and the health of mothers and infants. The temazcal, a traditional steam bath, was central to her cult. It was seen as a symbolic womb, a place of purification and rebirth, and rituals performed within it were believed to have healing and spiritual benefits. The festival of Ochpaniztli, held in the eleventh month of the Aztec calendar, was dedicated to Toci and other earth goddesses. This festival involved sweeping rituals to cleanse the earth, offerings of food and flowers, and dances to honor the earth's fertility. This festival sometimes included human sacrifice, representing the shedding of the old to make way for new growth.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Toci's influence extends beyond the Aztec period, with echoes of her symbolism found in contemporary Mexican culture. Her association with healing and herbal medicine continues to inspire traditional healers. The respect for grandmothers and the importance of family lineage reflect her enduring legacy as "Our Grandmother." While formal worship of Toci has ceased, her association with the earth, fertility, and healing remains relevant. Modern interpretations of Aztec mythology often highlight Toci as a powerful representation of female strength, wisdom, and the nurturing power of the earth. Her image serves as a reminder of the deep connection between humanity and the natural world, and the importance of respecting the wisdom of elders.

shortDescription: Toci, 'Our Grandmother,' was a major Aztec mother goddess. She was associated with healing, fertility, midwifery, and the earth.
